speaker: interviewer
text: So here you are. You're a cutter in Los Angeles. And-- but obviously, you didn't stay a cutter for a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I hate the job. Because it was just like, like a mule or a horse. We had those long tables. And you had to put on the fabric on those tables and go back and forward, back and forward, and then cut it with electric machine, which is very dangerous. It's a big knife, goes up, down. But being on the, on the job, I had to make a living. I was getting pretty good wages. I was getting already $125 a week at that time. It was a lot of money. I was looking around, to see what else I can do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So on a weekend, I used to talk to people who were in business. And one suggested, why don't you try a market business-- grocery. I got together with another friend-- another friend, actually, it's acquaintance. I gave notice to the owner of the factory that I'm quitting because-- I had to tell him a white lie, he really liked me, helped me a lot out-- that I, I had to go back to New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I left that place. And I went in and I, I bought a market. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It used to be a Safeway market, an old Safeway market on Figueroa Street. At that time, the end of the freeway used to be on Santa Barbara-- Santa Barbara-- street. It was the end of the freeway. They were building it. And I know once-- at the time, we used to come out. And we used to be close to 42nd Street. So we were a few blocks away from the freeway, so always used to be busy. And I told my partner, look, if they open up the freeway, we're going to lose a lot of business. And we had the big market already. We had-- I was at the register. We had two butchers. We had a liquor department-- a regular supermarket, an old Safeway supermarket. And I got out. And I got in a different business. I, I keep switching. I never stayed still in one place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the final business from which you retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had the liquor store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that located?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Culver City. We used to call it Culver Liquors Incorporated. It used to be across the MGM studios. I got acquainted with the studio over there. I used to get a lot of business from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I bet you did. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know also how to get in over there. And at that time, they still had many lots in Culver City. Now it's only left the Sony Studios. But at that time, it was MGM studios. And if you remember many years ago, they used to make the films how the French undergrounds are fought in, in the Germans. And they used to be across the street from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever in one?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Were you ever in a movie? No, no, never. I want no part of it. I was taking care of just customers. That's all it was about. Well, this is my last question. And then we'll go on to your photographs and your life. Now that it's 50-- over 50 years since the war is over, what is it that you want to pass on to your children and your grandchildren about what happened to you? What do you want them to remember? What's most important?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, they got to know about it, what happens in the past, what happens to my-- to our family or my family. And they should remember what human beings can do to human beings, and free them-- [SOBS] this is the most important thing in life. And they should always be a advocate and help somebody else who is in need. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] And it's unfortunate, but the human race should remember what happened to our Jewish people. And it shouldn't happen to any minority or group. All human beings have to live in peace. It's so many years. And I could never cry. It's the first time in my life, in over 52 years. It's, it was locked up in my mind or my body, but it's coming out. I hope it will help also somebody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much. And if you remember Ivan's last name, it would be nice. Are we going to do the pictures or his wife? OK, we're going to bring your wife on in a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shall I call her?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just sit still--no, just sit still for a moment. OK, Mr. Gradow, I see you have your-- a lovely woman sitting here with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll let you introduce us.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's my wife. Her name is Fanny Gradow. We've been married only 52 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: 42. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's going to say another 30 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: 42 Years. And we have two children, a son and a daughter. I think that-- you want to have the name too?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you gave us the names. But you have a simcha coming soon, don't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, our son is going to be married in March of '97. And we're looking forward to enjoy it. We met his bride, and she's a lovely girl. She was born in the United States. She's a teacher. And we hope to have some simcha. And also, we have two grandchildren from our daughter. The names, you know about it already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but you know, you didn't tell me the name of your son-in-law. So why don't you tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sorry. My-- it's Abbe Kingston.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's the-- going to be the name of your future daughter-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: It will be the same as my son's.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, but she has a first name.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you see, it's Eastern.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she's Michelle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Michelle, Eastern. That's her last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eastern, yeah, her last name is now Eastern, Michelle Eastern. She's from Portland, Oregon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're going to go up there for the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. I wouldn't miss it for anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that I have-- the two of you are sitting here together, and I know that this is difficult, but is there anything together that you want your children-- that you want to pass on? I know that you, Mr. Gradow, have already told us some things that you feel. And is there anything you and your wife, that you've ever discussed, that you want them to remember about--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I discussed it this morning. [SOBS] My daughter said something to me this morning that I can't forget it. She said, Mom, you inspired me, such Judaism, that I cannot-- most of her kids, they are in Santa Barbara. They are hippie types, people. They don't send kids to Hebrew school. So she says, but I am so glad that even my big girl said to me, Mom, I got to be-- a, this one, my

speaker: interviewee
text: Bat mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bat mitzvah. I have to have a bat mitzvah. So it made me feel real good. Because she feels like Saturday she has a special type of clothe that I went to Israel that I got her. My own granddaughter said, Mom, did you wash it out? Friday night. And the kids, you know, the little ones, two and a half year, [INAUDIBLE] he says to me, shabbat shalom. It really did to me. Not only what we did you know, with-- tough, it was not easy for them to become you know, professional people. It was not easy for us to, to do all the stuff for them. But I am very proud of them. We went to my son in Baltimore. This was my proudest day of my life. Because he had a very tough time with school. And I never forget that he's going to make it. And I inspired to them, you have to. There's no way of quitting here. And he never quit. And now he's a-- he's a, what is he, a director from a investment company. So and I knew it. I used to dream about that, that he is going to make it. There's no way out. We don't quit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, even with this profession that he has, I inspired him. Because he went for doctor, and he couldn't make it. So I said, if you grandfather would be alive, you know what he would do with you? You're good in math. That's where you go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, he the next day changed majors and he was on the dean's list. He was just going to drop out. But that word that I told him, it inspired them. A lot of things that I did, you know, from my upbringing. It helped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr Gradow, do you have anything you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all I can add on, that the kids should remember what we went through and guide their own children. They should be decent human beings. They should remember Judaism, because it's very important. They don't have to be orthodox. But they should bring up the children and grandchildren in our own ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think that concludes this part of the interview. We have some photographs. I want to thank you so much, so much, for sharing so much of your life with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. The picture you see over there, in front of you, on the left is my father, Nochim [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was taken approximately in the 1930s. I'm not sure of the date. Is it '32 or '34 or before. To his right is my uncle, Avram or Abraham. It's taken at the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- you should tell us their last names.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same name as-- the name is Gradowicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city and country was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the city of Mlawa. It's Poland, it's the western part of Poland. It's-- To my right, you see my father, [NON-ENGLISH] Nochim Gradowicz. It's my father. To the left, is my mother, Faiga Gradowicz. Her maiden name is [NON-ENGLISH]. It's made in, in Mlawa, in the, in the, in the '30s, the early '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Mlawa, Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland-- Mlawa, Poland, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the 1930s?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's made in 1930s.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I just need to know. This photograph and the first photograph, where did you obtain these? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Those pictures were in New York. Cousins of mine had it. In fact, one of those old pictures have still the handwriting from my parents. The picture, which-- in front of me is made in Berlin, in the French sector, in 1946. On the left, it's me. On the right is a friend of mine. We used to be in the same DP camp. But I do not remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you should tell us your full name, even then.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Jeffrey Gradow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it Gradow then? Or was it Gradowicz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, no. No, I'm sorry. At that time, I was still Jeff-- Jefim Gradowicz. The picture in front of me is our wedding picture on our wedding night, in New York City in the Bronx. In New York City in the Bronx, state of New York. My wife's name is Fanny Gradow. And I am Jeffery Gradow. We got married March 27, 1955.

speaker: interviewee
text: '54. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Cause you're married 42 years. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. It's-- The picture in front of me is on the right, my daughter, Helene Gradow. Next to it is my wife, Fanny Gradow. Next to it is my son, Steven Gradow, at his bar mitzvah in 1969. And next to it is me, a person enjoying the bar mitzvah of my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this-- where was the bar mitzvah taking--

speaker: interviewee
text: The bar mitzvah was, was in Mo gen David, on Pico Boulevard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city and state was it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: City of Los Angeles, state of California. This picture is from my son Steven Gradow, received today by express, from Baltimore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- but when do you think the photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- close to a park-- close to a park where he lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was yet in 198-- '96. This picture was taken in Santa Barbara, California. On the left, is my daughter Helene Gradow-- Kingston-- Gradow-Kingston. On the right is her husband, Abbe Kingston. And their children, my granddaughter Sarah and my grandson Elliot. It's taken in 1995, in the state of California.

speaker: interviewer
text: Santa Barbara.

speaker: interviewee
text: Santa Barbara. And I think this is the last photograph. And this is the last photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it gives me one more opportunity to thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much to you.
